sections.heading: Background: The Robber Council and the Crisis of Christology
sections.html:
Background: The Robber Council and the Crisis of Christology

The Tome of Leo — properly the Epistola dogmatica ad Flavianum, Leo I's dogmatic letter to Flavian, Bishop of Constantinople — is the single most influential piece of Christological writing produced between the Council of Nicaea (325) and the Protestant Reformation. Written in June 449 and formally received and ratified at the Council of Chalcedon in 451, it supplied the conceptual vocabulary and the theological framework that enabled the church's definitive statement of who Jesus Christ is.

To understand the Tome one must understand the crisis it addressed. The Council of Ephesus (431) had condemned Nestorianism — the position, associated with Nestorius of Constantinople, that Christ's two natures were so distinct that they constituted two separate personal subjects. Ephesus affirmed that Mary could rightly be called Theotokos (God-bearer), because the one born of her was a single personal subject who was genuinely divine. But the manner of Ephesus's triumph created its own theological reaction.

A monk named Eutyches, archimandrite of a large monastery near Constantinople, drew from Ephesus what he believed was the logical conclusion: if Christ is one Person, and if that Person is the eternal Son, then after the Incarnation there can be only one nature — the divine. The human nature, while real in some sense before the union, had been absorbed into or transformed by the divine nature. Eutyches was willing to say that Christ was consubstantial with the Father but not that he was consubstantial with us — a formulation that effectively denied the full reality of Christ's humanity. This view, which opponents labeled Monophysitism or (more precisely) Eutychianism, implied that the suffering, hungry, weeping Jesus of the Gospels was not genuinely human in the full sense that we are human.

In 448, Eutyches was condemned at a local synod in Constantinople presided over by Flavian, the Bishop of Constantinople. Eutyches appealed to the other great sees, including Rome. Pope Leo I, a man of formidable theological and administrative ability, received Flavian's account of the affair and began preparing his response. But before he could act effectively, the Emperor Theodosius II convened a council at Ephesus in August 449 — a council whose proceedings would earn it the permanent name of Latrocinium (the Robber Council), the name Leo himself gave it in rage.

The Robber Council was presided over by Dioscorus, the powerful Archbishop of Alexandria, who was determined to vindicate Eutyches and assert Alexandrian theological and political dominance. The council was conducted under conditions of intimidation bordering on violence. Imperial soldiers and a mob of monks loyal to Dioscorus surrounded the meeting place. Flavian of Constantinople was physically assaulted — beaten so severely, tradition records, that he died of his injuries within days of the council's close. Flavian's Christological position was condemned, Eutyches was rehabilitated, and bishops who objected were silenced or expelled.

Most significantly, when Leo's representatives arrived at the Robber Council bearing the Tome — which he had written as his definitive theological statement for the council's consideration — Dioscorus refused to allow it to be read. The letter was suppressed. Leo's rage was absolute. He wrote to the Emperor Theodosius II, to the Empress Eudoxia, to Pulcheria (the Emperor's devout sister), and to everyone of influence he could reach, demanding that the decisions of the Robber Council be overturned and a genuine council convened.

The political situation changed with the death of Theodosius II in July 450. His successor, Marcian, was a soldier-emperor far less sympathetic to Alexandrian theology and far more willing to work with Leo. Marcian and his wife Pulcheria (sister of the late Theodosius) convened the Council of Chalcedon for October 451. When the Tome of Leo was finally read aloud to the assembled bishops — the vindication of the document that had been suppressed two years before — the council broke into the famous acclamation: "Peter has spoken through Leo! This is the faith of the Fathers! This is the faith of the Apostles!"

sections.heading: The Two-Natures Doctrine: Key Passages
sections.html:
The Two-Natures Doctrine: Key Passages

The theological substance of the Tome is a sustained argument that the Incarnation does not result in the suppression or transformation of either nature — divine or human — but in their permanent union in the single Person of the eternal Son. Leo develops this argument with a clarity and precision that had not previously been achieved in the Latin West, drawing on the rich tradition of Latin theology (Tertullian, Ambrose, Augustine) and applying it to the precise points at issue with Eutyches.

Leo's central thesis is that each nature in Christ retains its own proper properties (proprietas) while being united with the other in the single Person of the Son. He states:

He who is true God is likewise true man. In this union there is no falsehood, because the humility of the manhood and the loftiness of the Godhead are found together. For as God is not changed by the compassion which He shows, so man is not consumed by the dignity which he receives... Each nature retains without defect its own proper character; and as the form of God does not take away the form of a servant, so the form of a servant does not impair the form of God.

This is the essential principle: the Incarnation is a union, not a fusion. The divine does not absorb the human; the human does not diminish the divine. Each remains what it essentially is, while the two are permanently and inseparably joined in the one Person.

Leo then proceeds to a celebrated passage in which he distinguishes the human and divine operations in the Gospel narratives — not to divide Christ into two persons acting independently, but to demonstrate that both natures remain fully operative and that the single Person acts through both:

To hunger, to thirst, to be weary, and to sleep is clearly human. But to satisfy five thousand men with five loaves, to bestow living water upon the Samaritan woman, to walk upon the surface of the sea with unwet feet, and by rebuking the storm to lay the boisterous waves — these are clearly divine. As therefore to pass over many instances, it does not belong to the same nature to weep with tears of compassion over a dead friend, and to call him back to life again with a word of power after the stone was removed from the grave of four days.

This passage illustrates what theologians call the communicatio idiomatum (the communication of attributes): because the two natures are united in the single Person of the Son, the properties and actions of each nature can be attributed to that one Person. It is accurate to say that "God was born," "God died" — because the Person who was born and died is the eternal Son, even though birth and death in the strict sense are attributes of his human nature. The Tome gave this principle its clearest early articulation.

Leo anchors his Christology directly in the Johannine and Pauline testimony. The prologue of John — "the Word became flesh" — is his constant reference point: the Incarnation is a genuine becoming, not an appearance; the Word genuinely took up human nature, and that nature is genuinely flesh, genuinely human, genuinely mortal. (John 1:14; Phil. 2:5–8; Heb. 4:15; Rom. 9:5)

The Philippians passage — the kenosis hymn — is particularly important for Leo. Christ, "who, though he was in the form of God, did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied himself, by taking the form of a servant" (Phil. 2:6–7) — this is the pattern of the Incarnation. The "emptying" (kenosis) does not mean that the divine nature was diminished or that the Son ceased to be God; it means that the eternal Son added human nature to his divine nature, entering into the conditions of human existence. He who was in the form of God took the form of a servant — both forms genuine, both retained.

On the human side, Leo is equally insistent. Christ was "in all things like unto us, without sin" (Heb. 4:15) — not merely appearing to suffer, not merely seeming to be tired, but genuinely experiencing the conditions of creaturely human life. The tears at Lazarus's tomb were real tears. The cry of desolation from the cross was a real cry. Docetism — the view that Christ only appeared to suffer — is as much an error as Eutychianism. Both deny the full reality of one of the natures.

sections.heading: Reception at Chalcedon and Ongoing Significance
sections.html:
Reception at Chalcedon and Ongoing Significance

The Council of Chalcedon (451) adopted the Tome of Leo not by incorporating it verbatim into the Chalcedonian Definition but by using its conceptual framework and vocabulary to shape the Definition's language. The influence is unmistakable. The Chalcedonian Definition's four famous adverbs — inconfusedly, unchangeably, indivisibly, inseparably — directly encode Leo's insistence that the two natures are neither confused (i.e., merged into one) nor divided (i.e., split into two persons). The Definition's insistence that "the distinction of natures is by no means taken away by the union, but rather the property of each nature is preserved" is Leo's language.

The council explicitly endorsed the Tome as a standard of orthodox teaching. The assembled bishops declared it to be "consistent with the confession of the great Peter" and conformable to the teaching of Cyril of Alexandria — a crucial point, since Cyril had been the great champion of Nicene theology at Ephesus, and his orthodoxy was beyond question. By aligning Leo with Cyril, Chalcedon presented the Definition as the synthesis of the Alexandrian and Roman theological traditions, not the repudiation of either.

The reception of the Tome was not, however, universal. The Oriental Orthodox churches — the Coptic (Egyptian), Syriac (Antiochene), and Armenian churches — rejected Chalcedon's Definition and, with it, Leo's Tome. These churches held that the orthodox position was that of Cyril at his most rigorous: one nature of the incarnate Word (mia physis tou theou logou sesarkōmenē). In their view, Chalcedon's "two natures" language dangerously approached Nestorianism, and Leo's influence made matters worse, since Leo was a Latin Western theologian working in a different theological idiom than the Greek-speaking Cyrillians. The Oriental Orthodox churches remain in separation from both Rome and Constantinople to this day on this basis, though ecumenical dialogue in the twentieth century has produced tentative agreements suggesting that the Chalcedonian and non-Chalcedonian traditions may be confessing the same reality in different philosophical vocabularies.

Leo's papalism is a second, entirely distinct dimension of the Tome's historical significance. The acclamation of the bishops — "Peter has spoken through Leo!" — was not merely an honor paid to a fine theological document; it reflected Leo's own self-understanding and his theological claims. Throughout his pontificate (440–461), Leo articulated with greater clarity than any previous bishop of Rome the doctrine that the Bishop of Rome exercises the authority of Peter in an ongoing and authoritative way — not merely as Peter's successor in terms of occupying the same see, but as the one in whom Peter's unique authority (principatus) continues to be operative. The Tome carries this claim implicitly: when Leo writes, Peter speaks.

This claim was resisted by the Eastern bishops at Chalcedon itself. Canon 28 of Chalcedon, which Leo protested vigorously, granted Constantinople "equal privileges" to Rome — not on the basis of Petrine authority but on the basis of political reality: Constantinople was the imperial capital. The tension between Leo's Petrine theory of Roman primacy and the East's resistance to that theory foreshadows the Great Schism of 1054 and remains a central issue in Catholic-Orthodox ecumenical dialogue.

For the history of Christian thought, the Tome of Leo is the most important single document in Christological history between the Nicene Creed and the Reformation. It supplied what Nicea had not: not merely the assertion that Christ is fully divine (homoousios with the Father) but a framework for understanding how divinity and humanity coexist in the single Person of the Son without confusion, without change, without division, and without separation. The fullness of the Godhead dwells bodily in Christ — neither a diminished deity nor a compromised humanity, but both in their fullness, united forever in the incarnate Son. (Col. 2:9; 1 Tim. 2:5)
